capacity_headline.note: 4.9 GWh commissioned in 2025 alone — matching the entire cumulative output from 2017 to 2024 combined

fleet_milestones.event: Hornsdale Power Reserve commissioned (100 MW / 129 MWh)
Victorian Big Battery commissioned (300 MW / 450 MWh)
Torrens Island BESS commissioned (250 MW / 250 MWh)
Fleet approaches 2 GW at end of 2024
Fleet crosses 3 GW — record monthly revenue (winter demand + transmission outages)
Waratah Super Battery partial operations (350 MW / 750 MWh of 850 MW / 1,680 MWh)
Fleet reaches 5 GW; Melbourne RE Hub A3 — NEM's first 4-hour battery; Eraring Battery Stage 1 (460 MW)
Supernode Stage 1 commercial operations (260 MW / 619 MWh)
Fleet exceeds 8 GW / 16.6 GWh — 53 registered units

major_projects.name: Waratah Super Battery
major_projects.note: World's most powerful battery; Powin battery systems

major_projects.name: Eraring Battery Stage 1
major_projects.revenue_model: Merchant + portfolio optimisation with Eraring coal
major_projects.note: Largest in southern hemisphere; Wartsila systems; adjacent to 2.88 GW coal plant closing late 2027

major_projects.name: Orana BESS
major_projects.revenue_model: CIS underwriting + 12-yr virtual offtake with EnergyAustralia (200 MW)

major_projects.name: Riverina 1 + Darlington Point
major_projects.note: Three co-located batteries; Riverina 1 was Q1 2026 capture rate leader

pipeline.name: Tomago BESS
pipeline.note: Fluence systems; next to aluminium smelter

pipeline.name: Richmond Valley BESS
pipeline.note: 8-hour duration — longest in NEM; NSW LDS tender winner; Hanwha batteries

pipeline.name: Merino Solar + Battery
pipeline.note: CIS generation tender winner; hybrid 450 MW solar

pipeline.name: Calala BESS
pipeline.note: First merchant BESS non-recourse debt in NSW ($260m senior debt)

nsw.warning: NSW warned that 75% of its 56 GWh storage requirement for 2030 has not yet reached FID

major_projects.name: Supernode Stage 1
major_projects.note: CATL batteries; near Brisbane data centre

major_projects.name: Western Downs BESS Stages 1-2
major_projects.revenue_model: Virtual toll agreements with AGL, Shell, Engie
major_projects.note: Tesla Megapack; co-located with major solar farm

major_projects.name: Bouldercombe Battery
major_projects.note: Near Gladstone; still ramping trading sophistication

pipeline.name: Supernode Stages 2-3
pipeline.note: Total project: 780 MW / 3,074 MWh (expandable to 1 GW+ / 5 GWh)

pipeline.name: Borumba Pumped Hydro
pipeline.note: 24-hour storage; flagship QLD energy transition project

qld.note: QLD BESS capacity grew 2.7x YoY while energy-only revenues dropped 73%. QLD is the leading indicator for cannibalisation across the NEM.

major_projects.name: Melbourne Renewable Energy Hub A3
major_projects.note: NEM's first 4-hour battery; showing early signs of superior spread capture vs 2-hour fleet

major_projects.name: Victorian Big Battery
major_projects.note: Q1 2026 capture rate: 2nd in NEM; Moorabool

major_projects.name: Mortlake BESS
major_projects.revenue_model: Portfolio optimisation with Mortlake gas (566 MW)
major_projects.note: Victoria's second-largest; adjacent to largest gas plant

vic.note: VIC revenue essentially flat YoY. Q1 2026 price spreads fell to $86/MWh. Battery fleet set prices above $300/MWh slightly more often, but that role was divided by more assets.

major_projects.name: Torrens Island BESS
major_projects.note: $28M EBITDA in first 9 months; hit by AEMO directions Nov 2025 + Mar 2026; Wartsila systems

major_projects.name: Hornsdale Power Reserve
major_projects.note: 7 years of optimisation; third-highest monthly revenue ever in Aug 2025 (Heywood outage); Tesla Autobidder

major_projects.name: Blyth BESS
major_projects.note: Lost ~12.7% of annual discharge to curtailment in 2025

pipeline.name: Bungama BESS
pipeline.note: First fully merchant bank-financed battery in NEM; milestone for investor confidence

sa.note: SA revenue climbed 49% YoY (Oct 25 - Mar 26). Q1 2026 price spreads $328/MWh — 4x QLD's $78/MWh. Aug 2025: SA batteries earned $563k/MW/yr, 84.5% from Lower 1-second FCAS. Heywood interconnector outage forced local FCAS procurement. Resilience driven by limited interconnection — but fleet growth means this is unlikely to be repeated.

tas.note: TAS has minimal grid-scale BESS. Pumped hydro (existing Tarraleah + proposed Battery of the Nation) remains Tasmania's primary storage strategy.

summary.note: Batteries overtook hydro as the most frequent price-setting technology in Q1 2026

time_of_day.evening_peak_note: Battery discharge reached 359 MW average in Q1 2026 (up from 98 MW in Q1 2025 — 3.7x). BESS set evening prices up to 50% of the time — twice as often as prior year
time_of_day.midday_note: Battery charging absorbs midday oversupply, pushing daytime prices UP. Average charging increased by 872 MW in Q1 2026 vs prior year
time_of_day.overnight_note: Gas peakers displaced from traditional evening price-setting role; coal increasingly irrelevant in shoulder periods
time_of_day.spread_compression: NEM-wide battery price spreads declined from $183/MWh in Q1 2025 to $121/MWh in Q1 2026 — a 34% compression

by_month.note: Record month — coal outages + cold snap drove extreme prices; some batteries earned 3-4x normal
SA outlier: $563k/MW/yr from FCAS; other states ~$96k average
Doubled from Dec — heatwave price spikes
Record low since Modo index began (Jul 2022); 19% MoM fall

nsw.note: Duration nearly doubled — partially offset revenue decline

qld.note: Worst-performing state; went from NEM's top earner to worst in a single year. Paradoxically, QLD batteries still had highest capture rates due to consistent (if small) arbitrage conditions

vic.note: Essentially flat YoY; battery fleet set prices above $300/MWh slightly more often but role divided by more assets

sa.note: Revenue climbed 49% YoY; spread 4x QLD. Driven by Heywood outages + FCAS scarcity. 'Unlikely to be repeated in 2026' as fleet grows

qld_collapse_detail.note: QLD is 12-18 months ahead of NSW and VIC on the cannibalisation curve. The physics of arbitrage are the same everywhere — this pattern will repeat.

fcas_analysis.trend: Structurally saturated — declining across all regions
detail: FCAS accounts for only 9% of total NEM BESS revenue — down from ~30% in 2023
NSW and VIC: FCAS revenues now negligible
SA and QLD: Still some contingency value during islanding events, but declining
By 2030: >80% of battery revenues will come from energy arbitrage as FCAS saturates completely
Aug 2025 SA outlier: 84.5% of revenue from Lower 1-second FCAS — Heywood interconnector outage forced local procurement. Hornsdale recorded third-highest monthly revenue ever

fpp.note: Frequency Performance Payments replaced old Causer Pays allocation. Financial impact negligible so far.

by_quarter.note: Winter demand + coal outages drove extreme prices — masked cannibalisation

evidence: QLD 2-hour price spreads hit 'lowest in a number of years' over summer 2025-26
Revenue dropped from $86k (Oct) to $29k (May 2026) — still falling with no sign of stabilisation
Commodity price declines compound: gas prices down 9-17% YoY, coal down 12%, pulling down the evening price ceiling
The dynamic is self-reinforcing: more batteries → flatter spreads → lower revenue per MW → but batteries still needed for reliability, so deployment continues
Modo capture rates forecast to fall from 85% in 2026 to 58% by 2035
WattClarity warns: 'as more systems connect, logically these highly correlated operating patterns will weigh on financial performance across the fleet'
potential_stabilisers: Coal retirements (Eraring 2027, Yallourn 2028) will restore some price volatility — removing baseload removes the floor that compresses spreads
Data centre demand growth (4x by 2030) will increase overall load, creating new demand for evening/night capacity
SA industrial demand doubling 2029-2034 (hydrogen, minerals processing)
Longer duration batteries (4-8 hours) capture value in different time periods, partially escaping the 2-hour correlation penalty
Market Price Cap increase to $23,200/MWh from July 2026 increases upside potential during scarcity events
is_there_a_floor.wattclarity_battery_correlation_penalty: Highly correlated operating patterns (all charge midday solar trough, all discharge evening peak) structurally erode the spread they target. WattClarity coined 'battery correlation penalty' to describe this self-defeating dynamic.

top_performers_q1_2026.note: Federation Asset Management / Edify; consistent performance despite low spreads
Neoen; 7+ years of Autobidder optimisation; system security contracts supplement
QLD batteries had highest capture rates despite lowest absolute revenue

bidding_sophistication.capture_rate_note: Lowest in over 12 months — low volatility quarter left less room for sophistication to differentiate

trading_platforms.note: In-house platform; used across Tesla fleet + third-party assets; sub-5-minute rebid capability
~2,500 MW of wind/solar; optimises Akaysha Energy battery assets; 'crazy to try and bid without automation'
UK-origin optimiser with Melbourne office; expanding into NEM
Most battery capacity owner-optimised; Neoen developed proprietary software for virtual battery dispatch in 5-minute intervals

bidding_sophistication.rebidding_insight: Daily energy bids in the NEM increased from 5,000/day in 2021 to 30,000/day in 2025, driven overwhelmingly by batteries. Key Modo finding: 'Frequent rebidding does not result in higher capture rates' — revenue optimisation relies on forecast accuracy, not rebid frequency.

duration_advantage.four_hour_advantage: First 4-hour battery (Melbourne RE Hub A3, Dec 2025) showing early signs of superior spread capture. 4-hour systems achieve 13-15% IRR vs 2-hour at sub-10%. QLD longer-duration batteries recording highest capture rates.
duration_advantage.two_hour_outlook: Below 10% IRR as spreads compress; central scenario 5.5% or less. The 2-hour playbook is structurally challenged.
duration_advantage.eight_hour_emerging: Richmond Valley (275 MW / 2,200 MWh, 8-hour) under construction for 2027 — testing whether extreme duration captures overnight and multi-day volatility

bidding_sophistication.insight: In Q1 2026, optimisation quality mattered less than in volatile quarters — autobidder convergence occurred. When spreads are thin, everyone struggles equally. The sophistication gap widens during volatile events (winter peaks, coal outages, interconnector trips) when elite operators capture 2-3x the spread of passive participants.

april_2026_collapse.note: Victoria collapsed to $35.68/MWh in April 2026 — lowest mainland price

negative_prices.note: Q1 2026 improvement partly because battery charging absorbs midday oversupply, reducing negative price frequency

structural_drivers: Gas generation at lowest level since 1999
Grid-scale and distributed solar both set quarterly generation records in Q1 2026
Forward prices trading 12-18% below long-term averages
Gas prices down 9-17% YoY, coal down 12% — evening price ceiling lowering

modo_projections.note: Modo projects energy arbitrage drives >95% of forecast merchant revenues over asset life. 4-hour duration is now minimum viable for investment.

demand_growth: Data centres: 4x by 2030 — the single largest new demand driver
SA industrial demand: doubles 2029-2034 (hydrogen, minerals processing)
Overall NEM demand: up 8% by 2035 vs previous forecasts
Behind-the-meter battery installations: 219,000 systems (4.7 GWh) since July 2025 federal subsidy — WattClarity asks 'when will behind-the-meter start eating into the market's dinner'

coal_retirements.impact: Largest generator in NEM — removal creates material price volatility and evening peak scarcity
Removes reliable baseload from Victoria's already-tight supply

forward_outlook.structural_shift: Batteries are transitioning from high-margin opportunistic assets to essential infrastructure with utility-like returns. ~50% of offtake arrangements now structured as virtual tolls, revenue-share, or firmed PPAs. Approximately one-third of battery capacity uses government contracts (CIS, LTESA, VRET). The era of $150k+/MW/yr merchant revenue is over — but the era of contracted infrastructure returns is beginning.

rule_changes.detail: Raised from $20,300/MWh to $23,200/MWh — increases upside potential during scarcity events
AEMO directed AGL's Torrens Island battery in Nov 2025 and Mar 2026, constraining operations. Compensation for 'out of market actions' remains uncertain — regulatory risk for battery operators.

key_insights: The NEM commissioned 4.9 GWh of BESS in 2025 alone — matching the entire cumulative output from 2017 to 2024 combined. The fleet more than doubled in 12 months.
BESS revenue cannibalisation is accelerating with no clear floor — each new GW compresses returns for all participants. Revenue dropped from $86k to $29k/MW/yr in 7 months.
QLD is the canary: 2.7x capacity growth drove 73% revenue decline. Price spreads fell from $244/MWh to $78/MWh. NSW and VIC are 12-18 months behind on the same curve.
SA is the resilient outlier — revenue UP 49% YoY driven by Heywood interconnector outages forcing local FCAS procurement. But this is structural luck, not repeatable as fleet grows.
The sophistication gap narrowed in Q1 2026 — in a low-volatility quarter, autobidder convergence meant everyone struggled equally. The gap widens during volatile events.
4-hour batteries are now the minimum viable investment (8-14.5% IRR). 2-hour systems are below 10% IRR and structurally challenged. 8-hour is emerging (Richmond Valley 2027).
FCAS is structurally saturated at 9% of total revenue and declining. Energy arbitrage will drive >95% of future battery revenues.
Coal retirements (Eraring 2027, Yallourn 2028) are the key potential stabiliser — removing baseload should restore some price volatility that cannibalisation has crushed.
WattClarity's 'battery correlation penalty': all batteries charge midday solar trough, all discharge evening peak. This self-defeating correlation structurally erodes the very spread they target.

The business model is shifting: from merchant opportunism to contracted infrastructure. ~50% of offtake now structured as virtual tolls or revenue-share. Government contracts (CIS/LTESA) underpin ~1/3 of fleet capacity.
